# Exploring barriers to accessing health care services by young women in rural settings: a qualitative study in Australia, Canada, and Sweden

[*BMC Public Health*](/)
**volume 25**, Article number: 213 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

4754 Accesses

4 Citations

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2/metrics)

## Abstract

### Background

The aim of this study is to explore young rural women’s perceived barriers in accessing healthcare services with a focus on the interrelation between three marginalization criteria: age (youth), gender (female), and place of residence (rural areas) in Australia, Canada, and Sweden.

### Methods

Using a qualitative interpretive approach, we conducted semi-structured in-depth interviews with 31 young women aged 18 to 24 in selected rural communities. Data collection took place from May 2019 to January 2021, and the qualitative data were analyzed using NVivo software.

### Results

Self-perceived barriers for access to healthcare services among young women living in rural and remote areas encompass various challenges across individual, institutional, and structural levels. Individual barriers include limited knowledge about available health services, negative attitudes toward healthcare, psychological discomfort when seeking assistance, and economic affordability issues. Institutional challenges involve limited healthcare resources, gender insensitivity among providers, judgmental attitudes from healthcare staff, inadequate time management of services, and a lack of privacy and confidentiality within facilities. Structural barriers further compound these issues through socio-cultural and gender norms, insufficient coverage of universal health insurance, low budget allocations for health facilities in rural and small urban areas, and the geographic distance to healthcare providers. Addressing these multifaceted barriers is crucial to improving healthcare access for rural population.

### Conclusions

Appropriate strategies and policies must be introduced to promote access to healthcare services in rural and remote areas even in most high-`income countries.

[Peer Review reports](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2/peer-review)

## Background

Despite decades of research, improving access to equitable, efficient, and appropriate healthcare services remains a central concern in health sciences literature [[1](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR1 "Caffery L. Rural health equity: A case study: CQUniversity; 2021."), [2](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR2 "Atere-Roberts J, Smith JL, Hall IJ. Interventions to increase breast and cervical cancer screening uptake among rural women: a scoping review. Cancer Causes Control. 2020;31(11):965–77.")]. Notably, the World Health Organization defines “access” as meaning that people can receive a full range of quality health services that are geographically within reach, financially affordable, and culturally appropriate [[3](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR3 "Harrold J, Jackson LA. Hurry up and wait. The experiences of young women in rural Nova Scotia accessing specialized care. Can J Rural Med. 2011;16(3):83–8.")]. However, due to the relative subjectivity of the concept, research studies use a range of definitions to define access for diverse demographics in different geographic contexts [[4](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR4 "Levesque J-F, Harris MF, Russell G. Patient-centred access to health care: conceptualising access at the interface of health systems and populations. Int J Equity Health. 2013;12(1):18.")]. Scholars commonly analyse self-perceived health needs to determine the barriers people experience in accessing healthcare services, particularly in marginalized populations such as people living in remote settlements, ethnical/racial minorities, youth, and women [[3](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR3 "Harrold J, Jackson LA. Hurry up and wait. The experiences of young women in rural Nova Scotia accessing specialized care. Can J Rural Med. 2011;16(3):83–8."), [5](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR5 "Sibley LM, Glazier RH. Reasons for self-reported unmet healthcare needs in Canada: a population-based provincial comparison. Healthc Policy. 2009;5(1):87–101.")].

Various studies confirm that female gender negatively affects access to healthcare services [[6](#ref-CR6 "Braveman P. Health disparities and health equity: concepts and measurement. Annu Rev Public Health. 2006;27:167–94."),[7](#ref-CR7 "Whitehead M. The concepts and principles of equity and health. Health Promot Int. 1991;6(3):217–28."),[8](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR8 "Hart LG, Larson EH, Lishner DM. Rural definitions for health policy and research. Am J Public Health. 2005;95(7):1149–55.")]. Literature indicated that young women aged 15 and 24 years tend to experience more health disparities in accessing healthcare services due to their transitional status as older teenagers and/or young adults [[9](#ref-CR9 "Reid S. The rural determinants of health: using critical realism as a theoretical framework. Rural Remote Health. 2019;19(3):5184."),[10](#ref-CR10 "Afifi RA, Parker EA, Dino G, Hall DM, Ulin B. Reimagining rural: shifting paradigms about health and well-being in the rural United States. Annu Rev Public Health. 2022;43:135–54."),[11](#ref-CR11 "Hulme J, Dunn S, Guilbert E, Soon J, Norman W. Barriers and facilitators to family planning access in Canada. Healthc Policy. 2015;10(3):48–63."),[12](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR12 "Leipert BD. Rural Women’s Health Issues in Canada: An Overview and Implications for Policy and Research. CWS/cf. 2005;24(4). [cited 2025 Jan. 14]. Available from: 
                  https://cws.journals.yorku.ca/index.php/cws/article/view/6074
                  
                .")]. Additionally, since fewer young adults under 25 are well-established in their careers, youth often have a lower socio-economic status and lower income—further criteria independently associated with poorer health outcomes and access [[13](#ref-CR13 "Ryan KD. Health and sustainability of rural communities. Rural Remote Health. 2004;4:242."),[14](#ref-CR14 "Hurlington KG. Something out of nothing? Place-based resilience in rural Canadian youth. PhD Theses, Trent University. 2019. URL: 
                  https://batadora.trentu.ca/objects/etd-678
                  
                ."),[15](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR15 "Hankivsky O, De Leeuw S, Lee JA, Vissandjee B, Khanlou N. Health inequities in Canada intersectional frameworks and practices Vancouver. Vancouver, Toronto: UBC Press; 2011. URL: 
                  https://www.ubcpress.ca/asset/9407/1/9780774819756.pdf
                  
                .")]. A study in rural Australia found that a shortage of maternity healthcare providers could endanger the health of pregnant teenagers and their babies in this region [[14](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR14 "Hurlington KG. Something out of nothing? Place-based resilience in rural Canadian youth. PhD Theses, Trent University. 2019. URL: 
                  https://batadora.trentu.ca/objects/etd-678
                  
                .")]. Another study in northern rural Sweden highlights that youth-specified services offered in youth clinics are under-utilized and the youth-friendliness of these services can be improved by removing barriers related to payment, resolving communication difficulties with the health professionals—particularly for non-residents who may have language barriers, protecting patient privacy and confidentiality, and ensuring non-judgmental services [[15](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR15 "Hankivsky O, De Leeuw S, Lee JA, Vissandjee B, Khanlou N. Health inequities in Canada intersectional frameworks and practices Vancouver. Vancouver, Toronto: UBC Press; 2011. URL: 
                  https://www.ubcpress.ca/asset/9407/1/9780774819756.pdf
                  
                .")]. A study investigated self-perceived barriers and facilitators to reproductive healthcare services within the youth population and concluded that the availability and adequacy of reproductive healthcare services combined with increased knowledge about sexual health would improve the health status of young women through social empowerment [[16](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR16 "Lys C, Reading C. Coming of age: how young women in the Northwest Territories understand the barriers and facilitators to positive, empowered, and safer sexual health. Int J Circumpolar Health. 2012;71:18957.")].

On the other hand, studies consistently demonstrate that place of residence plays a dramatic role in people’s access to healthcare, particularly in small communities and rural setting that tend to be remote areas [[1](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR1 "Caffery L. Rural health equity: A case study: CQUniversity; 2021."), [3](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR3 "Harrold J, Jackson LA. Hurry up and wait. The experiences of young women in rural Nova Scotia accessing specialized care. Can J Rural Med. 2011;16(3):83–8."), [17](#ref-CR17 "Babitsch B, Gohl D, von Lengerke T. Re-revisiting Andersen’s behavioral model of health services use: a systematic review of studies from 1998–2011. Psychosoc Med. 2012;9:Doc11."),[18](#ref-CR18 "Thomas VG. Using feminist and social structural analysis to focus on the health of poor women. Women Health. 1994;22(1):1–15."),[19](#ref-CR19 "Petrie SJ. Addressing Rural Health Access Inequity by Assessing Potential and Organizational Readiness for Antifragile Electronic Health Project Design in Rural Communities: PhD Theses, Carleton University. 2022. URL: 
                  https://doi.org/10.22215/etd/2022-15095
                  
                ."),[20](#ref-CR20 "Lara D, Decker MJ, Brindis CD. Exploring how residential mobility and migration influences teenage pregnancy in five rural communities in California: youth and adult perceptions. Cult Health Sex. 2016;18(9):980–95."),[21](#ref-CR21 "Sethna CDM. Accidental tourists: Canadian women, abortion tourism, and travel. Women’s Stud. 2012;41(4):457–75."),[22](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR22 "Plunkett R, Leipert B, Ray SL, Olson JK. Healthy spaces in meaningful places: the rural church and women’s health promotion. J Holist Nurs. 2015;33(2):122–33.")]. Rural areas with relatively few inhabitants compared with metropolitan areas, tend to have fewer resources, reduced accessibility to healthcare services, and are more prone to acute and chronic health issues [[21](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR21 "Sethna CDM. Accidental tourists: Canadian women, abortion tourism, and travel. Women’s Stud. 2012;41(4):457–75.")]. In a study of rural healthcare access in Australia, it was shown that rural residents have greater rates of morbidity and mortality due to unique environmental hazards specific to their areas, conservative socio-cultural values, insufficient number of healthcare service providers, significant geographic distance to many healthcare services, and poorly distributed services; which limit access to healthcare services [[23](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR23 "Thomas SWJ, Humphreys J. What does it cost to provide equity of access to high-quality, comprehensive primary health care in rural Australia? A pilot study. Rural Remote Health. 2017;17:4019.")]. As a result, rural residents must often travel long distances to access necessary care [[24](#ref-CR24 "Pong RW PJ. Geographic distribution of physicians in Canada: beyond how many and where. Ottawa: Canadian Institute for Health Information; 2005. URL: 
                  https://publications.gc.ca/site/eng/9.650063/publication.html
                  
                ."),[25](#ref-CR25 "Goicolea I, Coe AB, San Sebastián M, Hurtig AK. Developing and sustaining adolescent-friendly health services: a multiple case study from Ecuador and Peru. Glob Public Health. 2017;12(8):1004–17."),[26](#ref-CR26 "Lombardo AP, Angus JE, Lowndes R, Cechetto N, Khattak S, Ahmad F, Bierman AS. Women’s strategies to achieve access to healthcare in Ontario, Canada: a meta-synthesis. Health Soc Care Community. 2014;22(6):575–87."),[27](#ref-CR27 "Johnston K, Harvey C, Matich P, Page P, Jukka C, Hollins J, Larkins S. Increasing access to sexual health care for rural and regional young people: similarities and differences in the views of young people and service providers. Aust J Rural Health. 2015;23(5):257–64."),[28](#ref-CR28 "Tylee A, Haller DM, Graham TM, Churchill RE, Sanci LA. Youth-friendly primary-care services: how are we doing and what more needs to be done? Lancet. 2007;369:1565–73."),[29](#ref-CR29 "Wong ST, Regan S. Patient perspectives on primary health care in rural communities: effects of geography on access, continuity and efficiency. Rural Remote Health. 2009;9(1):1142."),[30](#ref-CR30 "Ireland S, Belton S, Doran F. “I didn’t feel judged”: exploring women’s access to telemedicine abortion in rural Australia. J Prim Health Care. 2020;12(1):49–56."),[31](#ref-CR31 "Thomée S, Malm D, Christianson M, Hurtig AK, Wiklund M, Waenerlund AK, Goicolea I. Challenges and strategies for sustaining youth-friendly health services - a qualitative study from the perspective of professionals at youth clinics in northern Sweden. Reprod Health. 2016;13(1):147."),[32](#ref-CR32 "Dolan E, Allott K, Proposch A, Hamilton M, Killackey E. Youth access clinics in Gippsland: Barriers and enablers to service accessibility in rural settings. Early Interv Psychiatry. 2020;14(6):734–40."),[33](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR33 "Motamedi M, Shahbazi M, Merghati-Khoei E, Salehi M, Hajebi A, Rahimi-Naghani S, et al. Perceived barriers to implementing sexual health programs from the viewpoint of health policymakers in Iran: a qualitative study. Sex Res Soc Policy. 2021;19:273–85.")]. Ultimately, this lengthy travel combined with associated costs intensifies access barriers [[25](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR25 "Goicolea I, Coe AB, San Sebastián M, Hurtig AK. Developing and sustaining adolescent-friendly health services: a multiple case study from Ecuador and Peru. Glob Public Health. 2017;12(8):1004–17."), [30](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR30 "Ireland S, Belton S, Doran F. “I didn’t feel judged”: exploring women’s access to telemedicine abortion in rural Australia. J Prim Health Care. 2020;12(1):49–56."), [33](#ref-CR33 "Motamedi M, Shahbazi M, Merghati-Khoei E, Salehi M, Hajebi A, Rahimi-Naghani S, et al. Perceived barriers to implementing sexual health programs from the viewpoint of health policymakers in Iran: a qualitative study. Sex Res Soc Policy. 2021;19:273–85."),[34](#ref-CR34 "Kennedy EC, Bulu S, Harris J, Humphreys D, Malverus J, Gray NJ. “Be kind to young people so they feel at home”: a qualitative study of adolescents’ and service providers’ perceptions of youth-friendly sexual and reproductive health services in Vanuatu. BMC Health Serv Res. 2013;13(1):455."),[35](#ref-CR35 "Mwangakala HA. Pregnant women’s access to maternal health information and its impact on healthcare utilization behaviour in rural Tanzania. UK: University of Loughborough; 2016."),[36](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR36 "Ambresin A-E, Bennett K, Patton GC, Sanci LA, Sawyer SM. Assessment of youth-friendly health care: a systematic review of indicators drawn from young people’s perspectives. J Adolesc Health. 2013;52(6):670–81.")].

In light of this background, very few studies have explored perception and interpretations of young women’s experiences in these rural areas in accessing health care services in real life [[3](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR3 "Harrold J, Jackson LA. Hurry up and wait. The experiences of young women in rural Nova Scotia accessing specialized care. Can J Rural Med. 2011;16(3):83–8."), [37](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR37 "Roberts S, Graham M, Barter-Godfrey S. Young mothers’ lived experiences prior to becoming pregnant in rural Victoria: a phenomenological study. Aust J Rural Health. 2011;19(6):312–7.")]. Because access to healthcare is primarily subjective, exploring the perceptions of young women in rural areas can empower their voices and help tailor the healthcare system to better meet their actual needs. Thus, even in countries with well-established healthcare systems, understanding individuals’ perspectives and interpretations of the barriers to healthcare access is essential. This insight can be invaluable for improving access to healthcare and enhancing the universality of health systems in high-income countries. This study attempts to understand how young women remember and talk about their experiences when accessing healthcare services in their rural communities in Australia, Canada and Sweden. What are the self-perceived barriers to access and use of healthcare services? How do young women get informed about available healthcare services?

### The present study

This study selected three high-income countries, Australia, Canada, and Sweden to explore barriers to healthcare access among women in rural areas. These countries have high-quality healthcare systems, diverse populations, and well-established infrastructure with reliable data on the provision of healthcare services. In addition, they have stable political systems, ensuring policies related to healthcare provision are less likely to be disrupted during the study. Despite similarities in the healthcare systems, there are differences, providing an opportunity to identify best practices that can be shared between countries. The focus of this paper is not on the health system per se but rather on the experience of young women with health delivery and access in rural settings. In that sense, the minor system differences at the macro level are not as influential as the lived realities of residents. Overall, these countries provide a good balance between diversity, data availability, and stability for a comparative healthcare study [[35](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR35 "Mwangakala HA. Pregnant women’s access to maternal health information and its impact on healthcare utilization behaviour in rural Tanzania. UK: University of Loughborough; 2016."), [38](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR38 "Jones CL, Jensen JD, Scherr CL, Brown NR, Christy K, Weaver J. The Health Belief Model as an explanatory framework in communication research: exploring parallel, serial, and moderated mediation. Health Commun. 2015;30(6):566–76.")].

Canada, Sweden, and Australia also have single-payer healthcare systems; consequently, each country strives to deliver equitable access to healthcare services through their administration of a public healthcare system. In Canada, the Health Act of 1984 guarantees the provision of essential medical services through provisional health insurance plans that cover associated healthcare costs. Nevertheless, many healthcare costs remain uncovered, including some private services and some medications. In Canada, some paramedical services are privatized completely while others are covered only for certain populations based on eligibility criteria, including age and income [[39](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR39 "Government of Canada. Canada’s health care system. Ottawa (CAN)2019.")]. Consequently, many Canadians have employer-provided extended benefit plans to supplement these coverage gaps. Similarly, local and territorial governments in Australia provide standard health services free of charge for residents. However, lack of coverage for some services and medications similarly necessitates private health insurance plans to bridge gaps in coverage [[40](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR40 "Australian Government. The Australian health system Canberra (AU):2019.")]. Similar to Canada, Australia subsidizes medications only for certain sub-populations—such as children and low-income seniors—through their “Medicare Benefits Schedule” and “Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme” [[40](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR40 "Australian Government. The Australian health system Canberra (AU):2019.")]. In Canada, each provincial healthcare plan has its criteria for subsidizing medication costs; however, most provinces do subsidize prescribed medications for children, seniors, and low-income families. Finally, Sweden also provides government-funded healthcare services, but the program was decentralized according to the Health and Medical Service Act of 1982. Consequently, governments for each of Sweden’s 290 municipalities and 21 regional councils make healthcare decisions for their respective local areas [[41](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR41 "Tikkanen R OR, Mossialos E, Djordjevic A, Wharton GA.,. International health care system profiles. Sweden, New York: New York: The Commonwealth Fund; 2020.")]. Although private healthcare options exist in Sweden, private practitioners must be contracted by cottage hospitals, known as *sjukstugor*. These cottage hospitals provide primary care for an annual out-of-pocket payment of 1150 SEK (~ USD 112.42); after this individual limit has been reached, healthcare services are then provided free of charge [[41](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR41 "Tikkanen R OR, Mossialos E, Djordjevic A, Wharton GA.,. International health care system profiles. Sweden, New York: New York: The Commonwealth Fund; 2020."), [42](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR42 "Swedish Healthcare. Swedish healthcare: all you need to know. The Local; 2013.")].

On the surface, these countries seem to have well-structured healthcare systems with advanced medical technology; nevertheless, rural populations—particularly young women—experience disparities in accessing healthcare services [[26](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR26 "Lombardo AP, Angus JE, Lowndes R, Cechetto N, Khattak S, Ahmad F, Bierman AS. Women’s strategies to achieve access to healthcare in Ontario, Canada: a meta-synthesis. Health Soc Care Community. 2014;22(6):575–87."), [35](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR35 "Mwangakala HA. Pregnant women’s access to maternal health information and its impact on healthcare utilization behaviour in rural Tanzania. UK: University of Loughborough; 2016."), [36](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR36 "Ambresin A-E, Bennett K, Patton GC, Sanci LA, Sawyer SM. Assessment of youth-friendly health care: a systematic review of indicators drawn from young people’s perspectives. J Adolesc Health. 2013;52(6):670–81."), [43](#ref-CR43 "Hankivsky O, Doyal L, Einstein G, Kelly U, Shim J, Weber L, Repta R. The odd couple: using biomedical and intersectional approaches to address health inequities. Glob Health Action. 2017;10(sup2):1326686."),[44](#ref-CR44 "Jaworsky D, Logie CH, Wagner AC, Conway T, Kaida A, de Pokomandy A, et al. Geographic differences in the experiences of HIV-related stigma for women living with HIV in northern and rural communities of Ontario, Canada. Rural Remote Health. 2018;18(3):4522."),[45](#ref-CR45 "Thiede M, McIntyre D. Information, communication and equitable access to health care: a conceptual note. Cad Saude Publica. 2008;24(5):1168–73."),[46](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR46 "Lizardo O. Beyond the antinomies of structure: Levi-Strauss, Giddens, Bourdieu, and Sewell. Theory Soc. 2010;39(6):651–88.")]. According to the Australian Institute of Health and Welfare (2018), inadequate access to healthcare services in rural and remote areas in Australia has created “medically under-served” populations with higher death rates, and lower life expectancies compared to bigger metropolitan cities. Thus, although access barriers are a global health problem, this study is significant in its finding that high income countries including Canada, Australia, and Sweden similarly experience substantial challenges in supplying appropriate, high-quality healthcare services across smaller communities [[47](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR47 "Farmer JMS, Threlkeld G. Theory in rural health. Aus J Rural Health. 2012;20(4):185–9.")]. Rural populations in these countries have lower socio-economic status, fewer career opportunities, and inequitable access to health services compared with those living in urban settings [[47](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR47 "Farmer JMS, Threlkeld G. Theory in rural health. Aus J Rural Health. 2012;20(4):185–9."), [48](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR48 "Malatzky C, Gillespie J, Couch DL, Cosgrave C. Why place matters: a rurally-orientated analysis of COVID-19's differential impacts. Social Sciences and Humanities Open. 2020;2(1):Article number: 100063.")].

### Theoretical considerations

This study employed the Healthcare Access Barriers model in order to identify barriers to accessing the health system among young women in rural areas in different domains: individual, institutional, and structural. The HealthCare Access Barrier framework [[49](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR49 "Carrillo JE, Carrillo VA, Perez HR, Salas-Lopez D, Natale-Pereira A, Byron AT. Defining and targeting health care access barriers. J Health Care Poor Underserved. 2011;22(2):562–75.")] identifies various types of barriers to accessing healthcare: financial, organizational, geographic, cultural, and personal. In this study, we assumed rural women may face financial barriers due to a lack of health insurance coverage or inability to pay for healthcare services. Organizational and geographic barriers may also limit accessibility to healthcare services. Cultural barriers may affect Indigenous women in Australia and Canada due to historical trauma, cultural misunderstandings, and discrimination. Personal barriers related to their health awareness and information about the services, social stigma, fear of judgment, and lack of trust in healthcare providers may also affect healthcare utilization [[3](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR3 "Harrold J, Jackson LA. Hurry up and wait. The experiences of young women in rural Nova Scotia accessing specialized care. Can J Rural Med. 2011;16(3):83–8."), [50](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR50 "Goicolea I, Carson D, San Sebastian M, Christianson M, Wiklund M, Hurtig A-K. Health care access for rural youth on equal terms? A mixed methods study protocol in northern Sweden. Int J Equity Health. 2018;17(1):6.")]. These aspects guided the interviews’ topic guide. The investigator explored the perspective and interpretations of participants on barriers to accessing health care services at various personal, organizational, financial, and geographical domains in their natural contexts in a more flexible manner. In addition, the intersection of these factors was considered in understanding the access barriers of rural young women living in Australia, Canada, and Sweden.

### Study aims and objectives

This study aimed to explore the personal, institutional, and structural barriers that young women perceive and experience in accessing healthcare services in rural Australia, Canada, and Sweden and how assigns meaning to these experiences. Finally, we aim to provide recommendations to enhance healthcare access for young women in rural settings.

## Methods

Given the nature of the study’s aims and objectives, a qualitative approach was selected. In this approach, the epistemological underpinning is that knowledge is socially constructed as people interact with each other and with internal and external stimuli, respectively. Consequently, the study’s philosophical approach relies on a basic interpretive approach that examines women’s experiences in accessing healthcare services in rural areas [[43](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR43 "Hankivsky O, Doyal L, Einstein G, Kelly U, Shim J, Weber L, Repta R. The odd couple: using biomedical and intersectional approaches to address health inequities. Glob Health Action. 2017;10(sup2):1326686.")].

The main researcher (the first author) lived in these places for extended periods. By being fully immersed in the environment where one is acquiring knowledge, researchers can begin to understand connections and relationships that would be difficult to grasp without close connection with the environment [[51](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR51 "Saunders B, Sim J, Kingstone T, Baker S, Waterfield J, Bartlam B, et al. Saturation in qualitative research: exploring its conceptualization and operationalization. Qual Quant. 2018;52(4):1893–907."), [52](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR52 "Merriam SB. Introduction to qualitative research. In: Merriam SBGR, editor. Qualitative research in practice: examples for discussion and analysis. San Francisco (USA): Jossey-Bass; 2002. p. 3–17.")].

When conducting research in rural communities, it’s essential to partner with the communities and develop comprehensive approaches to the problems they face. Therefore, relying solely on virtual meetings, reports, or similar materials can undermine the value of the novel observations gained [[51](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21387-2#ref-CR51 "Saunders B, Sim J, Kingstone T, Baker S, Waterfield J, Bartlam B, et al. Saturation in qualitative research: exploring its conceptualization and operationalization. Qual Quant. 2018;

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

SUMMARY:

# FIVE BARRIERS TO RURAL HEALTHCARE ACCESS

## The wall between your facility and your community

Timely access to quality healthcare services has a profound effect on every aspect of a person’s health. However, rural residents, who account for almost 19.3% of the US Population, are facing inequalities when it comes to both access and quality of healthcare.

Rural healthcare facilities are left to bear the burden of caring for these unique communities. Solutions that get carried over from urban areas are proven to be, not only ineffective, but detrimental as they fail to address the unique barriers to healthcare access of rural America.

### The [Five](https://www.ruralhealthinfo.org/topics/healthcare-access) Barriers to Rural Healthcare Access:

![](https://myabmed.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/The-Barriers-1200x600-768x384-3.png)
![](https://myabmed.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/Workforce-shutterstock_1051689107-Converted-01-150x150-3.png)

#### Workforce Shortages

Less than [8%](https://www.ruralhealthinfo.org/topics/health-care-workforce#hpsa) of all physicians and surgeons choose to practice in rural setting. The workforce shortages extend to all healthcare professionals from medical management to health aides and it is one of the more significant barriers to quality healthcare access. The shortages create an enormous burden to both the facilities and the communities. The facilities are facing increasing costs from constant recruiting efforts. Facilities are having difficulty maintaining quality from high turnaround and unfilled positions. More importantly, facilities lose their community’s trust.

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20150%20150'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://myabmed.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/Access-shutterstock_516800032-Converted-01-150x150-3.png)

#### Distance, Transportation & Internet

Geographic access is another rural healthcare access barrier. 97% of the country’s land mass is rural but only [19.3%](https://www.ruralhealthweb.org/about-nrha/about-rural-health-care) of the population lives there. Sparsely populated areas result in longer travel distances to access healthcare. The travel burden is amplified by the road conditions, the terrain and the weather. The distance-to-provider is also affected by the ability of the rural residents to secure transportation. Socioeconomic factors limit the access to reliable private transportation and most rural communities luck adequate public transportation making accessing healthcare very difficult.

The luck of high-speed internet with a reliable connection is another factor widening the distance-to-provider. [39%](https://www.cnet.com/news/life-in-the-slow-lane-welcome-to-the-internet-in-rural-america/) of rural residents do not have access to high-speed broadband internet. This translates to an inability to research nearby healthcare facilities, plan routes and even worse, rural residents are denied access to telemedicine services that could provide a solution to the geographic limitations.

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20150%20150'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://myabmed.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/Insurance-shutterstock_577768111-Converted-01-300x300-22.png)

#### Health Insurance Coverage

[9.1%](https://www.ruralhealthinfo.org/topics/healthcare-access) of residents leaving outside metropolitan statistical areas, are without any type of health insurance. Rural residents, when compared to their urban counterparts, face higher health insurance premiums, difficulty affording their co-pays, less access to low-cost or charity healthcare as well as less providers to choose from in their participating plan. As a result, rural residents are delaying or even forgoing seeking healthcare.

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20170%20125'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://myabmed.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/Privacy-shutterstock_1329018929-Converted-01-300x221-22.png)

#### Social Stigma and Privacy Issues

[Anonymity, privacy and social stigma](https://www.ruralhealthinfo.org/topics/healthcare-access) are significantly tougher barriers to overcome in rural communities. Rural residents can feel apprehensive in seeking healthcare because of lack of privacy and anonymity. Family, friends and co-workers can easily find out when a resident seeks medical attention, either due to direct observation or because they are working in the healthcare facilities themselves. The problem intensifies when rural residents are seeking care for conditions associated with social stigma such as mental health, substance abuse, sexual health, etc.

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20150%20150'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://myabmed.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/Privacy-shutterstock_1557362978-300x300-22.png)

#### Poor Health Literacy

Rural residents have a higher risk for low [health literacy](https://www.ruralhealthinfo.org/topics/social-determinants-of-health#literacy-and-education), because on average, they have lower education levels, they are living in poverty or do not have English proficiency. Low health literacy not only affects the health choices an individual makes, but also their ability to understand and follow healthcare instructions or navigate the complex healthcare system.

### Supporting the Rural Facilities

Rural healthcare facilities are losing the battle with providing care to their communities while keeping their doors open. The new emerging barrier to healthcare access is the closure of rural facilities or the discontinuation of services. [41%](https://revcycleintelligence.com/news/41-of-rural-hospitals-operating-with-negative-margins) of rural hospitals operate at a negative margin and [161](https://www.shepscenter.unc.edu/programs-projects/rural-health/rural-hospital-closures/)rural hospitals have closed since January 2005. Each time a rural facility downsizes or closes, there are severe consequences that are felt throughout the local and surrounding communities. Apart from the obvious loss of healthcare services, there is loss of jobs, loss of tax revenue, loss of supporting business and loss professional residents.

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20768%20384'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://myabmed.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/Negative-Margin-1200x600-768x384-2.png)

[AB Med](https://myabmed.com/) believes that the solutions need to work for both sides the facility and the community. We believe in Healthy Facilities, Healthy Communities! Contact us to learn how we can assist your facility in providing the healthcare quality that your rural community deserves! [Let’s Connect.](https://myabmed.com/contact/)

We at [AB Med](https://myabmed.com/services/) are excited to talk to you, [contact us](mailto:info@myabmed.com?subject=Better%2520Healthcare%2520for%2520Rural%2520Communities)

Rural Health is our passion and focus

enriched_points.expanded_content: Geographic distance to healthcare facilities disproportionately affects rural populations, limiting access to essential services and exacerbating health disparities.
Many rural residents face higher health insurance premiums and limited options, leading to delayed or forgone care due to affordability concerns.
Inadequate public transportation and long travel distances hinder access to healthcare, especially for those without reliable private transport.
Limited healthcare providers in rural areas result in extended wait times for appointments, impacting timely medical intervention and patient outcomes.
Rural areas often experience shortages of healthcare professionals, reducing the availability of critical services and contributing to poorer health outcomes.
